definitions.definition: Safeguarding the interests and rights of consumers against unethical and unfair trade practices by sellers.
A person who buys goods or hires services for personal use, not for resale or commercial purpose. Defined under Consumer Protection Act 2019.
Protection against goods hazardous to life and property. Consumers should buy ISI-marked and quality-certified products.
Right to complete information about quality, quantity, price, ingredients, manufacturing and expiry dates on the product label.
Freedom to select from a variety of goods and services at competitive prices without any pressure.
Consumer's interests will receive due consideration in appropriate forums, consumer councils, and government policies.
Right to fair settlement of genuine grievances — replacement, removal of defect, or compensation through consumer courts.
Right to know about consumer rights, responsibilities, and remedies available. Includes awareness campaigns.
First tier of consumer redressal. Handles complaints up to ₹1 crore. Headed by President at District Judge level.
Second tier. Handles complaints from ₹1 crore to ₹10 crore. Also hears appeals against District Commission orders.
Highest tier. Handles complaints above ₹10 crore. Hears appeals against State Commission. Located in New Delhi.
Indian Standards Institute (BIS) certification for industrial products like electrical appliances, cement, LPG cylinders.
Agricultural Marketing certification for agricultural products like spices, honey, ghee, and pulses.
Fruit Products Order certification for processed fruit products like jams, juices, squashes, and pickles.
BIS certification for gold and jewellery products, ensuring purity and quality.

distinctions.between: District Commission vs State Commission vs National Commission

points.a: District Judge level / High Court Judge level

scenarios.scenario: An electric heater with ISI mark causes a short circuit and damages furniture within a week of purchase.
scenarios.principle: Product hazardous to life and property despite quality mark

scenarios.scenario: A medicine bottle has no expiry date, no ingredients list, and no dosage information.
scenarios.principle: Consumer entitled to complete product information on label

scenarios.scenario: A shopkeeper forces a customer to buy a specific brand of flour and refuses to sell any other brand.
scenarios.principle: Freedom to select from a variety without pressure

scenarios.scenario: Ramesh buys a defective laptop worth ₹80,000 and wants to file a complaint.
scenarios.principle: First tier handles complaints up to ₹1 crore

scenarios.scenario: A builder sells a flat worth ₹5 crore with construction defects. The buyer files a complaint.
scenarios.principle: Second tier handles complaints from ₹1 to ₹10 crore

scenarios.scenario: An airline delays a flight causing ₹15 crore loss to a business group.
scenarios.principle: Highest tier for complaints above ₹10 crore

scenarios.scenario: A consumer buys gold earrings but later discovers the purity is less than what was claimed.
scenarios.principle: BIS Hallmark ensures gold purity and quality

scenarios.scenario: A company runs misleading advertisements claiming its product cures diabetes.
scenarios.answer: Unfair Trade Practice — ground for complaint

scenarios.scenario: A consumer awareness group files a complaint on behalf of thousands of affected consumers.
scenarios.principle: Recognised consumer associations can file on behalf of consumers

scenarios.scenario: The government runs 'JAGO GRAHAK JAGO' advertisements on TV and radio to educate consumers.
scenarios.principle: Government campaign spreading awareness about consumer rights
